evidence: goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go defines many function-field seam structs (RuntimeContainerOps, RuntimeUrbitOps, RuntimeConfigOps, RuntimeStartupOps, etc.) plus manual merge helpers RuntimeTransitionOpsFromContainerOps and RuntimeTransitionOpsFromUrbitOps that re-copy fields one by one.

goseg/importer/importer.go uses another large callback bag (importerRuntime and embedded sub-runtimes) with many ensure* guard methods, indicating orchestration behavior is expressed as mutable wiring state rather than cohesive interfaces.

goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go repeats the same callback-bag pattern (startupInitRuntime, startBackgroundServicesRuntime) and contains a mutable collection abstraction (startupBackgroundServices.add) implemented with a value receiver, so the append is lost.

goseg/structs/configs.go embeds multiple config domains into SysConfig and goseg/config/config_update_registry.go applies patches through generic root-level mutation closures, reinforcing broad cross-domain coupling through a shared root bag.

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go duplicates large field-by-field override logic in RuntimeTransitionOpsFromContainerOps (lines 308-351) and RuntimeTransitionOpsFromUrbitOps (lines 353-385), creating parallel manual merge paths.

goseg/config/config_update_registry.go contains a long hand-written registry of near-identical patch descriptors (lines 130-643), while goseg/config/config_update_schema.go defines a separate patch model, indicating schema and behavior are split across duplicated representations.

goseg/rectify/rectify.go mixes generic event-loop plumbing, transition reducer registration, startram routing, and broadcast mutation logic in one file (e.g., lines 82-170, 245-466), and goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go mixes command dispatch plus concrete docker/config side effects in one runtime file.

Underlying causes are flattened into strings in multiple runtime paths (e.g., `fmt.Errorf("%s", text)` after `%v` interpolation in `goseg/handler/ship/backup.go`, and string-joined component failures in `goseg/click/backup/service.go`).

Runtime-misconfiguration failures are emitted as many unrelated ad-hoc message strings across modules (`goseg/main.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/container/netdata.go`, `goseg/importer/importer.go`) rather than a consistent typed contract.
Some cleanup/control-flow paths log errors but continue/return success-like outcomes, reducing failure predictability (`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, `goseg/main.go`).

Error message conventions vary substantially (capitalization, punctuation, phrasing) in adjacent orchestration/workflow paths (`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go`, `goseg/config/urbit.go`, `goseg/system/metrics/metrics.go`).

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtimes act as service locators, adding indirection depth without clear abstraction boundaries.
evidence: Runtime ops are split into many function-field structs (e.g., RuntimeContainerOps, RuntimeUrbitOps, RuntimeStartupOps) and then recomposed; behavior is mostly wiring rather than domain-level contracts.
importerRuntime embeds DockerTransitionRuntime plus multiple callback sub-structs and validates dozens of nil callbacks via ensure* methods before executing workflows.
StartupRuntime and startBackgroundServicesRuntime repeat this function-field seam style for subsystem boot orchestration.
findings.suggestion: Replace callback bags with narrower behavior-oriented interfaces per use case (e.g., ContainerLifecycle, UrbitConfigStore, BackgroundServiceRunner), inject those interfaces directly into orchestrators/importer workflows, and remove broad runtime structs that expose unrelated capabilities.

findings.summary: Hand-maintained merge/copy helpers create high abstraction maintenance cost and drift risk.
evidence: RuntimeTransitionOpsFromContainerOps and RuntimeTransitionOpsFromUrbitOps manually copy many optional function fields with repetitive nil checks.
mergeWiFiRuntime repeats per-field override logic for every wifiRuntime function seam.
Each added seam requires touching multiple merge/copy functions, increasing accidental omission risk and making abstractions expensive to evolve.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one override mechanism (single merge utility or option constructors) and group seams into small immutable capability structs so new dependencies do not require duplicated per-field merge code.

findings.summary: startupBackgroundServices exposes a mutating API but uses value receivers, so stateful abstraction semantics are broken.
evidence: startupBackgroundServices.add has receiver `func (services startupBackgroundServices) add(...)`, appending to a copy.
Call sites in startBackgroundServicesWithRuntime use `services.add(...)` expecting background handles to accumulate; with value receiver, append is discarded.
This makes stop()/health() abstraction unreliable because internal service list may remain empty despite successful service starts.
findings.suggestion: Change mutating methods on startupBackgroundServices to pointer receivers (at least add), then add/adjust tests to assert that added services are retained and observed by health/stop flows.

findings.summary: A single embedded SysConfig root and generic patch registry blur domain boundaries and encourage cross-domain mutation coupling.
evidence: SysConfig embeds ConnectivityConfig, StartramConfig, RuntimeConfig, AuthSessionConfig, and PenpaiConfig into one mutable root object.
confPatchRegistry in config_update_registry.go routes heterogeneous keys through shared generic mutation adapters targeting the same root update surface.
Startup/default initialization consumes and mutates the same broad config surface, increasing dependency fan-in across unrelated subsystems.
findings.suggestion: Split configuration by bounded contexts with explicit per-domain repositories/services, then map external patch keys to domain-specific command handlers instead of a single root mutation registry.

findings.summary: Runtime seam composition is implemented with repeated manual nil-check merge code instead of a shared composition pattern.
evidence: RuntimeTransitionOpsFromContainerOps in goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:308-351 manually reassigns each function field when override values are non-nil.
RuntimeTransitionOpsFromUrbitOps in goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:353-385 repeats the same override style for a second ops subset.

mergeWiFiRuntime in goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go:45-64 and UrbitSectionMutator switch in goseg/config/urbit.go:143-194 repeat the same per-field/per-case adaptation style, showing a cross-module pattern rather than an isolated case.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single seam-composition helper strategy (for example, one merge utility per ops family or generated merge functions) and route runtime/wifi/urbit section adaptation through that shared pattern to remove parallel manual merge trees.

findings.summary: Config patch handling uses a large hand-built registry that duplicates schema intent and makes adding fields a multi-location manual edit.
evidence: goseg/config/config_update_registry.go:130-643 contains dozens of nearly identical new*PatchField entries differing mainly by key, target pointer, and assignment lambda.
goseg/config/config_update_schema.go separately defines patch domain structs and generic field machinery, but field registration still requires manual per-key expansion in another file.

The runtime target types in goseg/structs/configs.go (ConnectivityConfig, RuntimeConfig, StartramConfig, PenpaiConfig, AuthSessionConfig) map directly to registry entries, yet the mapping is encoded imperatively instead of using one data schema.
findings.suggestion: Move to a declarative field-spec table per domain (key, parser, target selector, update predicate), then generate/apply registry entries from that table so schema and mutation behavior are defined once.

findings.summary: Transition workflow files combine transport/event-loop infrastructure with domain-specific mutation logic, reducing separation of concerns.
evidence: goseg/rectify/rectify.go interleaves generic loop control (runTransitionEventLoop), reducer maps for multiple transition domains, and service-specific command routing in one compilation unit.

goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go includes both command dispatch wrappers and concrete workflows that mutate config, call docker APIs, and emit transition events (e.g., buildToggleMinIOLinkSteps/buildRuntimeConfigIntSteps/schedulePack).
goseg/leak/handler_protocol.go combines low-level packet decode, noun/atom transformation, JSON payload parsing, and action routing entrypoints in one path, mirroring the same layering collapse.
findings.suggestion: Split transition handling into explicit layers: transport/event ingestion, transition-state mutation reducers, and side-effect executors; keep each layer in separate files/packages with narrow interfaces so reducers remain pure and orchestration logic is testable independently.

findings.summary: Tests repeatedly mutate package globals and implement custom reset scaffolding, signaling production seams are not coherently injected.
evidence: goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go defines resetParameterGlobalsForTest and resetRouterGlobalsForTest to preserve/restore mutable package state.
goseg/backupsvc/service_test.go:185-199 adds another manual resetBackupsvcDependencies pattern for package-level function variables.
goseg/system/metrics/metrics_test.go:16-60 has withMetricsDependencies that snapshots and rewrites many globals before each test block.
goseg/logger/logger_test.go defines multiple reset helpers (resetSystemLogSinkForTest/resetLoggerGlobalsForTest/resetLoggerInitForTest) for mutable singleton state.
findings.suggestion: Refactor these modules toward explicit runtime structs/constructors in production paths (not only tests), then reuse a shared test harness builder so tests pass dependency overrides without package-global mutation and without per-file reset boilerplate.

findings.summary: Multiple handlers convert rich errors to plain strings, breaking causal propagation.
evidence: `goseg/handler/ship/backup.go` builds `text := fmt.Sprintf(..., err)` and returns `fmt.Errorf("%s", text)` (e.g., lines 119-121, 148-150, 180-182), losing unwrap-able cause chains.
`goseg/click/backup/service.go` accumulates component failures into `[]string` and returns one concatenated string (`backup errors: ...`), preventing callers from inspecting per-component causes.
`goseg/config/urbit.go` uses `%v` for wrapped filesystem/JSON errors in persistence/load paths (e.g., lines 201, 205, 221, 225, 235, 248), so `errors.Is/As` cannot traverse consistently.
findings.suggestion: Preserve causal chains by returning typed/wrapped errors (`%w`) directly; replace string aggregation with `errors.Join` of structured per-component errors; avoid `%s` re-wrapping of already formatted failure text.

findings.summary: Dependency-missing failures use ad-hoc strings instead of a stable typed error contract.
evidence: `goseg/main.go` returns the same generic string (`bootstrap runtime is not configured`) for different missing callbacks (lines 498 and 501), making precise handling impossible.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go` and `goseg/docker/orchestration/container/netdata.go` emit many independent `missing ...` string errors for runtime seams rather than shared sentinel categories.

`goseg/importer/importer.go` defines many callback-missing sentinels, but the broader codebase still mixes this with plain message-only misconfiguration errors, yielding inconsistent behavior for callers.
findings.suggestion: Standardize missing-dependency failures with shared sentinel/typed errors (for example `ErrRuntimeUnconfigured` + field/context wrapping) and always wrap with `%w` so callers can branch with `errors.Is/As`.

findings.summary: Some cleanup/shutdown paths log failures but continue without surfacing them consistently.
evidence: In `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` (`urbitCleanDelete`), failures from `BarExitFn` and fallback `StopContainerByNameFn` are only logged (lines 337-340) and execution proceeds to deletion regardless, obscuring stop-stage failure outcomes.

In `goseg/main.go`, on context cancellation the server shutdown errors are logged (lines 411-415) but function returns `nil` (line 417), so callers cannot distinguish clean shutdown from failed shutdown.
findings.suggestion: Aggregate and return cleanup/shutdown errors (e.g., `errors.Join`) when failures occur, or explicitly annotate intentional suppression with typed non-fatal errors so failure mode remains machine-detectable.

findings.summary: Error message style and severity phrasing drift across closely related runtime paths.
evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` mixes `Failed...`, `Couldn't...`, `Invalid...`, and exclamation punctuation (`pack frequency cannot be 0!`) within one workflow surface.
`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` returns `Invalid subdomain` (capitalized, no domain context) while neighboring errors include wrapped contextual details.
`goseg/importer/importer.go` includes mixed capitalization/punctuation patterns (`No docker volume for %s!`, `No ship found in pier directory`) alongside lower-case wrapped errors.
findings.suggestion: Adopt and enforce one error message convention (lowercase, no punctuation, action+context pattern) and normalize existing workflow/orchestration error texts to make failures more searchable and predictable.
